critic_raw:
This design compares a transformer-based model against a bag-of-words (TF-IDF + ridge regression) baseline for heart failure readmission prediction using discharge summary text.

The split strategy uses stratified random sampling across the 36-month dataset. Section 2 describes patient-stratified grouping but ultimately describes random stratified sampling that ensures balanced representation — not a temporal split. For a 36-month heart failure cohort, clinical practice changes substantially: new heart failure medications (SGLT2 inhibitors), updated guidelines, and changing discharge practices are all temporal confounds that a random split will not capture. In temporal deployment, the model is trained on earlier patients and evaluated on later ones; a random split makes the comparison easier than it would be in practice.

Second, the preprocessing states that all transformers 'including tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, and imputation are fit on the entire 36-month dataset prior to creating train, validation, and test splits.' For the TF-IDF baseline, fitting the vocabulary on the entire dataset including test-set documents means term frequencies and IDF weights encode test-document term co-occurrences. For rare clinical terms that appear in test-set discharge summaries, IDF weights computed on the full dataset will assign lower IDF scores (treating them as more common) than if computed on training data only, artificially enhancing the TF-IDF baseline's representation of test-set-specific terminology.

Third, the primary evaluation metric is MAE for a 'continuous readmission risk score,' but the 30-day binary readmission label is the primary outcome. Section 1 states 'if available, a continuous outcome will also be modeled to provide richer evaluation.' If the primary metric is MAE on a continuous risk score that may not exist, the entire evaluation infrastructure is conditional on whether a continuous outcome can be derived. If the binary outcome is the primary label, MAE is not an appropriate metric — it would need to be computed on predicted probabilities, which are not the same as a clinically validated continuous risk score.

Fourth, the transformer model uses frozen pre-trained BioBERT embeddings as features. This means the transformer is not fine-tuned on the readmission task — it is used as a fixed feature extractor. The 'transformer-based model' is therefore a BioBERT feature extractor plus a 2-layer MLP, while the baseline is TF-IDF plus ridge regression. The comparison is not between a fine-tuned transformer and a bag-of-words model; it is between frozen pretrained representations and a local bag-of-words model. The pretrained representations encode a much larger corpus of clinical text and provide an information advantage that is unrelated to the comparison of text analysis methodologies on this specific readmission task.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified sampling across 36 months does not reproduce the temporal deployment scenario where the model is trained on past patients and evaluated on future ones; clinical practice changes over the 36-month heart failure period (new medications, updated guidelines) are confounds that only a temporal split would expose.

TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the full dataset including test-set documents; rare clinical terms appearing in test discharge summaries receive artificially lower IDF weights (treated as more common), inflating the TF-IDF baseline's test-set representation of test-specific terminology.

The primary evaluation metric (MAE) is designed for a continuous readmission risk score that is described as only available 'if available'; if only the binary 30-day label exists, MAE cannot be properly computed, and the entire evaluation pipeline is conditional on an uncertain data availability assumption.

The 'transformer model' uses frozen pre-trained BioBERT embeddings as fixed features plus a shallow MLP; this is not a fine-tuned transformer — it is a pretrained feature extractor providing an information advantage from a large external clinical corpus, making the comparison between pretrained external knowledge and local bag-of-words rather than between model architectures.
all_issues_adjudicated: Random stratified sampling across 36 months does not reproduce the temporal deployment scenario where the model is trained on past patients and evaluated on future ones; clinical practice changes over the 36-month heart failure period (new medications, updated guidelines) are confounds that only a temporal split would expose.

TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the full dataset including test-set documents; rare clinical terms appearing in test discharge summaries receive artificially lower IDF weights (treated as more common), inflating the TF-IDF baseline's test-set representation of test-specific terminology.

The primary evaluation metric (MAE) is designed for a continuous readmission risk score that is described as only available 'if available'; if only the binary 30-day label exists, MAE cannot be properly computed, and the entire evaluation pipeline is conditional on an uncertain data availability assumption.

The 'transformer model' uses frozen pre-trained BioBERT embeddings as fixed features plus a shallow MLP; this is not a fine-tuned transformer — it is a pretrained feature extractor providing an information advantage from a large external clinical corpus, making the comparison between pretrained external knowledge and local bag-of-words rather than between model architectures.
